title.zh: 卷三十九 蕭何曹參傳
title.en: Volume 39: Xiao He and Cao Shen
title.raw: 卷三十九 蕭何曹參傳 Volume 39: Xiao He and Cao Shen

sentences.zh: 蕭何，沛人也。

translations.literal: Xiao He was a man of Pei.
translations.idiomatic: Xiao He came from Pei.

sentences.zh: 以文毋害為沛主吏掾。

translations.literal: By being flawless in documents he became chief clerk of the chief administrator of Pei.
translations.idiomatic: Because his paperwork was impeccable, he was appointed chief clerk to Pei’s chief administrator.

sentences.zh: 高祖為布衣時，數以吏事護高祖。

translations.literal: When Gaozu was still a commoner, [He] repeatedly used official business as a pretext to shield Gaozu.
translations.idiomatic: While Liu Bang was still a commoner, Xiao He more than once used his office to shield him from trouble.

sentences.zh: 高祖為亭長，常佑之。

translations.literal: When Gaozu became a village head, [He] constantly assisted him.
translations.idiomatic: After Liu Bang became a village head, Xiao He habitually looked out for him.

sentences.zh: 高祖以吏繇咸陽，吏皆送奉錢三，何獨以五。

translations.literal: When Gaozu as an official performed corvée labor at Xianyang, the clerks all presented three hundred cash in parting gifts; He alone presented five hundred.
translations.idiomatic: When Liu Bang, as a conscripted clerk, went to Xianyang for corvée duty, his colleagues each gave him three hundred cash for the road; Xiao He alone gave five hundred.

sentences.zh: 秦御史監郡者，與從事辨之。

translations.literal: The Qin imperial clerk who supervised the commandery, together with his attendant, debated matters with him.
translations.idiomatic: The Qin censor who oversaw the commandery, and the clerk who served under him, took up administrative questions with him.

sentences.zh: 何乃給泗水卒史事，第一。

translations.literal: He was then assigned to the office of a clerk of the Si River commandery, and ranked first.
translations.idiomatic: He was posted as a clerk in the Sishui commandery office and placed first in the assessment.

sentences.zh: 秦御史欲入言徵何，何固請，得毋行。

translations.literal: The Qin censor wished to memorialize the throne and summon He for appointment; He firmly declined the request, and so was not required to go.
translations.idiomatic: The censor meant to recommend him to the court for appointment, but Xiao He held firm and was spared from having to go.

sentences.zh: 及高祖起為沛公，何嘗為丞督事。

translations.literal: When Gaozu rose as Duke of Pei, He ever served as assistant magistrate overseeing affairs.
translations.idiomatic: When Liu Bang was proclaimed Duke of Pei, Xiao He served as his assistant magistrate in charge of administration.

sentences.zh: 沛公至咸陽，諸將皆爭走金帛財物之府分之，何獨先入收秦丞相御史律令圖書臧之。

translations.literal: When the Duke of Pei reached Xianyang, the generals all raced to seize gold, silk, wealth, and goods from the treasuries and divide them; He alone went in first and collected the laws, orders, maps, and documents of the Qin chancellor and censor and stored them away.
translations.idiomatic: When the Duke of Pei entered Xianyang, his generals rushed to loot the treasuries; Xiao He alone went straight to the archives and secured the Qin government’s statutes, ordinances, maps, and registers.

sentences.zh: 沛公具知天下阨塞，戶口多少，彊弱處，民所疾苦者，以何得秦圖書也。

translations.literal: That the Duke of Pei fully knew where the strategic passes of the empire lay, how many households there were, where strength and weakness lay, and what the people’s hardships were, was because He obtained the Qin maps and documents.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Bang knew the lie of the land—its defiles, population, relative strength, and the people’s grievances—only because Xiao He had preserved the Qin maps and records.

sentences.zh: 初，諸侯相與約，先入關破秦者王其地。

translations.literal: At first the feudal lords mutually agreed that whoever first entered the Pass and destroyed Qin would be king of that territory.
translations.idiomatic: The rebel lords had agreed among themselves that whoever first broke through the passes and overthrew Qin would rule that territory.

sentences.zh: 沛公既先定秦，項羽後至，欲攻沛公，沛公謝之得解。

translations.literal: The Duke of Pei having first pacified Qin, Xiang Yu arrived afterward and wished to attack the Duke of Pei; the Duke of Pei apologized to him and the crisis was resolved.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Bang was first to subdue the Qin heartland; when Xiang Yu arrived later and meant to strike him, Liu Bang apologized and defused the threat.

sentences.zh: 羽遂屠燒咸陽，與范增謀曰：「巴蜀道險，秦之遷民皆居蜀。」

translations.literal: Yu then slaughtered and burned Xianyang, and consulted with Fan Zeng, saying, “The roads into Ba and Shu are perilous, and Qin’s banished people all dwell in Shu.”
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu then sacked and torched Xianyang. With Fan Zeng he reasoned, “The routes into Ba and Shu are treacherous, and Qin’s exiles have been settled in Shu.”

sentences.zh: 乃曰：「蜀漢亦關中地也。」

translations.literal: They therefore said, “Ba and Shu are also territory within the Pass.”
translations.idiomatic: So they declared, “Ba and Shu still count as land inside the passes.”

sentences.zh: 故立沛公為漢王，而三分關中地，王秦降將以距漢王。

translations.literal: Therefore they established the Duke of Pei as King of Han, while they divided the territory within the Pass into three parts and enfeoffed Qin’s surrendered generals as kings to block the King of Han.
translations.idiomatic: They named Liu Bang King of Han, carved the Guanzhong region into three kingdoms for Qin’s surrendered generals, and set those kings there to hem him in.

sentences.zh: 漢王怒，欲謀攻項羽。

translations.literal: The King of Han was angry and planned to attack Xiang Yu.
translations.idiomatic: Enraged, the King of Han prepared to march against Xiang Yu.

sentences.zh: 周勃、灌嬰、樊噲皆勸之，何諫之曰：「雖王漢中之惡，不猶愈於死乎？」

translations.literal: Zhou Bo, Guan Ying, and Fan Kuai all urged him on; He remonstrated, saying, “Though ruling Hanzhong is hateful, is it not still better than death?”
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Bo, Guan Ying, and Fan Kuai urged him to fight, but Xiao He objected: “Even Hanzhong is preferable to annihilation.”

sentences.zh: 漢王曰：「何為乃死也？」

translations.literal: The King of Han said, “How could that mean death?”
translations.idiomatic: The King of Han asked, “What makes you say that would mean our death?”

sentences.zh: 何曰：「今眾弗如，百戰百敗，不死何為？

translations.literal: He said, “Now our multitude does not match his; a hundred battles would mean a hundred defeats—if not death, then what?
translations.idiomatic: Xiao He replied, “Our forces cannot match his. Fight a hundred times and you lose a hundred times—what then but ruin?

sentences.zh: 周書曰『天予不取，反受其咎』。

translations.literal: The Zhou Documents say, ‘What Heaven grants, if you do not take, you will instead receive its blame.’
translations.idiomatic: The Documents of Zhou say, ‘What Heaven bestows, if you refuse it, you bring blame on yourself.’

sentences.zh: 語曰『天漢』，其稱甚美。

translations.literal: The saying calls it ‘the Han of Heaven’—the name is most auspicious.
translations.idiomatic: The very phrase ‘Han of Heaven’ is a splendid omen.

sentences.zh: 夫能詘於一人之下，而信於萬乘之上者，湯武是也。

translations.literal: To be able to humble oneself beneath one man and thereafter win trust from one who commands ten thousand chariots—that was Tang and Wu.
translations.idiomatic: Those who could bow to one man for a time and later command the allegiance of a ruler with ten thousand chariots were Tang and King Wu.

sentences.zh: 臣願大王王漢中，養其民以致賢人，收用巴蜀，還定三秦，天下可圖也。」

translations.literal: Your servant wishes the Great King to take his throne in Hanzhong, nurture the people to attract worthy men, gather Ba and Shu to his use, then return to settle the three Qin regions—the empire may yet be won.”
translations.idiomatic: I urge Your Majesty to accept Hanzhong, cherish the people and draw men of talent to you, draw on the resources of Ba and Shu, then strike east to secure the Three Qin—the whole realm can still be yours.”

sentences.zh: 漢王曰：「善。」

translations.literal: The King of Han said, “Good.”
translations.idiomatic: The King of Han said, “Well spoken.”

sentences.zh: 乃遂就國，以何為丞相。

translations.literal: He then proceeded to his kingdom, with He as chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: He went to his new domain and appointed Xiao He chancellor.

sentences.zh: 何進韓信，漢王以為大將軍，說漢王令引兵東定三秦。

translations.literal: He advanced Han Xin, and the King of Han made him grand general; [He] persuaded the King of Han to order him to lead the army east and settle the Three Qin.
translations.idiomatic: Xiao He recommended Han Xin, whom the King of Han named grand general, and persuaded him to send Han Xin east to conquer the Three Qin.

sentences.zh: 語在信傳。

translations.literal: The account is given in Han Xin’s biography.
translations.idiomatic: The full story is told in the biography of Han Xin.

sentences.zh: 何以丞相留收巴蜀，填撫諭告，使給軍食。

translations.literal: He, as chancellor, remained behind to gather Ba and Shu, pacify and instruct the people, and supply the army with provisions.
translations.idiomatic: As chancellor, Xiao He stayed behind to consolidate Ba and Shu, keep order, issue instructions, and provision the army.

sentences.zh: 漢二年，漢王與諸侯擊楚，何守關中，侍太子，治櫟陽。

translations.literal: In the second year of Han, the King of Han with the feudal lords struck Chu; He guarded Guanzhong, attended the heir apparent, and administered from Liyang.
translations.idiomatic: In the second Han year, while the King of Han campaigned against Chu with the allied lords, Xiao He held Guanzhong, attended the heir apparent, and ran the government from Liyang.

sentences.zh: 為令約束，立宗廟、社稷、宮室、縣邑，輒奏，上可許以從事；

translations.literal: He issued ordinances and regulations, established the ancestral temple, altars of soil and grain, palaces, and county seats, and for each matter submitted memorials; when the Sovereign approved, he might act accordingly;
translations.idiomatic: He promulgated laws and rules, founded the imperial temple, the altars of state, palaces, and county seats, and routinely memorialized the throne; whatever the Sovereign approved, he carried out;

sentences.zh: 即不及奏，輒以便宜施行，上來以聞。

translations.literal: If there was no time to memorialize, he would act on his own authority as expedient and report when the Sovereign came.
translations.idiomatic: when there was no time to wait for approval, he acted at his own discretion for the emergency and informed the Sovereign afterward.

sentences.zh: 計戶轉漕給軍，漢王數失軍遯去，何常興關中卒，輒補缺。

translations.literal: He counted households, rotated transport by water, and supplied the army; the King of Han repeatedly lost his army and fled, but He constantly raised troops in Guanzhong and filled the gaps.
translations.idiomatic: He levied taxes by household, ran the grain barges, and kept the armies fed; each time the King of Han’s host was shattered and he fled, Xiao He raised fresh levies in Guanzhong to replace the losses.

sentences.zh: 上以此剸屬任何關中事。

translations.literal: For this reason the Sovereign wholly entrusted all Guanzhong affairs to He.
translations.idiomatic: The Sovereign therefore left the entire administration of Guanzhong in Xiao He’s hands.

sentences.zh: 漢三年，與項羽相距京、索間，上數使使勞苦丞相。

translations.literal: In the third year of Han, [the King of Han] with Xiang Yu faced each other between Jing and Suo; the Sovereign repeatedly sent envoys to express sympathy for the toil of the chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: In the third Han year, locked with Xiang Yu between Jing and Suo, the Sovereign repeatedly sent messengers to “console” his weary chancellor.

sentences.zh: 鮑生謂何曰：「今王暴衣露蓋，數勞苦君者，有疑君心。

translations.literal: Bao Sheng said to He, “Now the King, exposing his person to wind and rain, repeatedly consoles your toil because he harbors doubts about you.
translations.idiomatic: Bao Sheng warned Xiao He, “The King endures hardship in the field yet keeps sending messengers to inquire after you—he is testing your loyalty.

sentences.zh: 為君計，莫若遣君子孫昆弟能勝兵者悉詣軍所，上益信君。」

translations.literal: For your sake I calculate: nothing would be better than to send your sons, grandsons, brothers, and cousins who can bear arms all to join the army in camp, so the Sovereign will trust you the more.”
translations.idiomatic: Send every able-bodied son, brother, and kinsman you have into his camp; only then will he truly trust you.”

sentences.zh: 於是何從其計，漢王大說。

translations.literal: Thereupon He followed his plan, and the King of Han was greatly pleased.
translations.idiomatic: Xiao He did so, and the King of Han was delighted.

sentences.zh: 漢五年，已殺項羽，即皇帝位，論功行封，群臣爭功，歲餘不決。

translations.literal: In the fifth year of Han, after Yu had been killed, he immediately assumed the imperial position; when discussing merit and conferring enfeoffments, the ministers wrangled over credit, and for more than a year no decision was reached.
translations.idiomatic: In the fifth Han year, with Xiang Yu dead, Liu Bang took the throne. Merit titles were debated, the ministers squabbled over precedence, and for more than a year nothing was settled.

sentences.zh: 上以何功最盛，先封為酇侯，食邑八千戶。

translations.literal: The Sovereign held He’s achievement the greatest, and first enfeoffed him as Marquis of Zan with an estate of eight thousand households.
translations.idiomatic: The Sovereign judged Xiao He’s service supreme and first enfeoffed him as Marquis of Zan with eight thousand households.

sentences.zh: 功臣皆曰：「臣等身被堅執兵，多者百餘戰，少者數十合，攻城略地，大小各有差。

translations.literal: The meritorious ministers all said, “We personally wore armor and grasped weapons; the more had over a hundred battles, the fewer several tens of engagements; we stormed cities and seized territory, each with his measure of achievement.
translations.idiomatic: The generals protested: “We wore armor and bore arms—some through a hundred fights, others dozens—we took cities and ground, each according to his deserts.

sentences.zh: 今蕭何未有汗馬之勞，徒持文墨議論，不戰，顧居臣等上，何也？」

translations.literal: Now Xiao He has had no sweat-of-the-horse toil; he merely holds brush and ink and discourses; he does not fight, yet looks down on us from above—why?”
translations.idiomatic: Xiao He never rode a charge or drew a bow; he only shuffled documents. How can he stand above us?”

sentences.zh: 上曰：「諸君知獵乎？」

translations.literal: The Sovereign said, “Do you gentlemen know hunting?”
translations.idiomatic: The Sovereign asked, “Do you know hunting?”

sentences.zh: 曰：「知之。」

translations.literal: They said, “We know.”
translations.idiomatic: They said they did.

sentences.zh: 「知獵狗乎？」

translations.literal: “Do you know the hunting dogs?”
translations.idiomatic: “And hunting dogs?”

sentences.zh: 曰：「知之。」

translations.literal: They said, “We know.”
translations.idiomatic: Again they said they did.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「夫獵，追殺獸者狗也，而發縱指示獸處者人也。

translations.literal: The Sovereign said, “In hunting, those who pursue and kill the beasts are the dogs; those who release the leash and point out where the beasts are are the men.
translations.idiomatic: The Sovereign said, “In a hunt, the hounds run down the game; the hunter slips the leash and shows them where to strike.

sentences.zh: 今諸君徒能走得獸耳，功狗也；

translations.literal: Now you gentlemen can only pursue and catch beasts—achievement of dogs;
translations.idiomatic: You only run the quarry down—you are the hounds’ share of the credit;

sentences.zh: 至如蕭何，發縱指示，功人也。

translations.literal: As for Xiao He, releasing the leash and pointing—achievement of men.
translations.idiomatic: Xiao He tracked the game and gave the signal—that is the hunter’s share.

sentences.zh: 且諸君獨以身從我，多者三兩人；

translations.literal: Moreover you gentlemen followed me with your persons alone, at most two or three men;
translations.idiomatic: Besides, you followed me with only your own persons, two or three at most;

sentences.zh: 蕭何舉宗數十人皆隨我，功不可忘也！」

translations.literal: Xiao He raised his whole lineage, several tens of men, all following me—his achievement must not be forgotten!”
translations.idiomatic: Xiao He brought his entire clan, dozens of kinsmen, into my service. Never forget that debt!”

sentences.zh: 群臣後皆莫敢言。

translations.literal: Afterward the ministers none dared speak.
translations.idiomatic: The assembly fell silent and dared not answer.

sentences.zh: 列侯畢已受封，奏位次，皆曰：「平陽侯曹參身被七十創，攻城略地，功最多，宜第一。」

translations.literal: When the ranked marquises had all received their enfeoffments and submitted the order of precedence, all said, “Marquis of Pingyang Cao Shen personally received seventy wounds in storming cities and seizing territory; his achievement was the greatest and he should be first.”
translations.idiomatic: When the enfeoffed lords drew up the roll of honor, they insisted that Cao Shen of Pingyang, with seventy wounds taken in siege and field, deserved first place.

sentences.zh: 上已橈功臣多封何，至位次未有以復難之，然心欲何第一。

translations.literal: The Sovereign had already indulged the meritorious ministers in heaping rewards on He, and as to the order of precedence had no further grounds openly to dispute them, yet in his heart he wished He to be first.
translations.idiomatic: The Sovereign had already yielded to his generals in lavishing titles on Xiao He; he could not easily overrule them on ranking, though he still wanted Xiao He first.

sentences.zh: 關內侯鄂千秋時為謁者，進曰：「群臣議皆誤。

translations.literal: Marquis Guannei E Qianqiu, then serving as usher, advanced and said, “The assembled ministers are mistaken.
translations.idiomatic: E Qianqiu, Marquis Guannei, then a court usher, stepped forward: “They are wrong.

sentences.zh: 夫曹參雖有野戰略地之功，此特一時之事。

translations.literal: Though Cao Shen has achievement in field campaigns and seizing territory, that is merely a moment’s work.
translations.idiomatic: Cao Shen’s battlefield conquests matter only for a single campaign.

sentences.zh: 夫上與楚相距五歲，失軍亡眾，跳身遯者數矣，然蕭何常從關中遣軍補其處。

translations.literal: When the Sovereign for five years faced Chu, losing armies and multitudes, several times leaping his person to escape, yet Xiao He constantly from Guanzhong sent troops to fill those places.
translations.idiomatic: For five years the Sovereign grappled with Chu, again and again shattered and fleeing alone, while Xiao He repeatedly refilled his ranks from Guanzhong.

sentences.zh: 非上所詔令召，而數萬眾會上乏絕者數矣。

translations.literal: Without an edict summoning them, yet several tens of thousands of men joined the Sovereign when supplies were exhausted—this happened several times.
translations.idiomatic: Tens of thousands marched to his relief without a summons whenever his supplies ran out—not once or twice.

sentences.zh: 夫漢與楚相守滎陽數年，軍無見糧，蕭何轉漕關中，給食不乏。

translations.literal: When Han and Chu held each other at Xingyang for years, the army had no grain in sight, yet Xiao He transported grain by water from Guanzhong and provisions did not fail.
translations.idiomatic: Years at Xingyang without grain in camp—yet Xiao He’s barges from Guanzhong never let the men go hungry.

sentences.zh: 陛下雖數亡山東，蕭何常全關中待陛下，此萬世功也。

translations.literal: Though Your Majesty repeatedly lost east of the mountains, Xiao He always preserved Guanzhong intact awaiting Your Majesty—this is achievement for ten thousand generations.
translations.idiomatic: You lost Shandong again and again, but Xiao He kept Guanzhong whole for your return—that is merit for the ages.

sentences.zh: 今雖無曹參等百數，何缺於漢？

translations.literal: Now though there were not a hundred men such as Cao Shen, what would Han lack?
translations.idiomatic: Had we lacked a hundred men like Cao Shen, would the dynasty have suffered?

sentences.zh: 漢得之不必待以全。

translations.literal: Han would not have needed them to be made whole.
translations.idiomatic: The house of Han would still have endured without them.

sentences.zh: 柰何欲以一旦之功而加萬世之功哉！

translations.literal: How then can you wish to place a single day’s achievement above achievement for ten thousand generations!
translations.idiomatic: How can you rank a day’s fight above a lifetime’s stewardship?”

sentences.zh: 蕭何當第一，曹參次之。」

translations.literal: Xiao He should be first; Cao Shen next.”
translations.idiomatic: Xiao He must be first; Cao Shen second.”

sentences.zh: 上曰：「善。」

translations.literal: The Sovereign said, “Good.”
translations.idiomatic: The Sovereign said, “Agreed.”

sentences.zh: 於是乃令何第一，賜帶劍履上殿，入朝不趨。

translations.literal: Thereupon he ordered He first, granting him the privilege of wearing a sword and shoes in the hall and entering court without hurrying.
translations.idiomatic: He ranked Xiao He first, allowed him to wear sword and shoes in the audience hall, and waived the rule that he must trot into court.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「吾聞進賢受上賞，蕭何功雖高，待鄂君乃得明。」

translations.literal: The Sovereign said, “I have heard that he who recommends the worthy receives the highest reward; though Xiao He’s achievement is high, it was through the Lord of E that it became clear.”
translations.idiomatic: He added, “They say recommending talent wins the highest reward. Xiao He’s greatness needed Lord E’s voice to be recognized.”

sentences.zh: 於是因鄂千秋故所食關內侯邑二千戶，封為安平侯。

translations.literal: Thereupon on the territory of two thousand households that E Qianqiu formerly held as Marquis Guannei, he was enfeoffed as Marquis of Anping.
translations.idiomatic: On the two-thousand-household Guannei estate E Qianqiu already held, he was now created Marquis of Anping.

sentences.zh: 是日，悉封何父母兄弟十餘人，皆食邑。

translations.literal: That day he enfeoffed all of He’s parents, brothers, and more than ten persons of his lineage, each with an estate.
translations.idiomatic: That same day he enfeoffed more than a dozen of Xiao He’s parents and brothers, each with a fief.

sentences.zh: 乃益封何二千戶，「以嘗繇咸陽時何送我獨贏錢二也」。

translations.literal: He additionally enfeoffed He by two thousand households, “because when I once performed corvée at Xianyang, He alone sent me two hundred cash extra.”
translations.idiomatic: He added two thousand households to Xiao He’s fief, “for the time when, bound for corvée at Xianyang, he alone gave me two hundred cash more than the others.”

sentences.zh: 陳豨反，上自將，至邯鄲。

translations.literal: When Chen Xi rebelled, the Sovereign personally led the army and reached Handan.
translations.idiomatic: When Chen Xi rose in revolt, the Sovereign took the field in person and marched to Handan.

sentences.zh: 而韓信謀反關中。

translations.literal: Meanwhile Han Xin plotted rebellion in Guanzhong.
translations.idiomatic: Meanwhile Han Xin plotted revolt inside Guanzhong.

sentences.zh: 呂后用何計誅信。

translations.literal: Empress Lü used He’s plan to execute Xin.
translations.idiomatic: Empress Lü used Xiao He’s stratagem to put Han Xin to death.

sentences.zh: 語在信傳。

translations.literal: The account is given in Han Xin’s biography.
translations.idiomatic: The details are recorded in Han Xin’s biography.

sentences.zh: 上已聞誅信，使使拜丞相為相國，益封五千戶，令卒五百人一都尉為相國衛。

translations.literal: When the Sovereign heard that Xin had been executed, he sent envoys to appoint the chancellor as minister of state and added five thousand households to his fief, ordering five hundred soldiers and one commandant of capital guards to guard the minister of state.
translations.idiomatic: Learning of Han Xin’s death, he promoted the chancellor to minister of state, added five thousand households to his fief, and assigned five hundred troops under a commandant of the capital guard to “protect” him.

sentences.zh: 諸君皆賀，召平獨弔。

translations.literal: The gentlemen all congratulated him; Zhao Ping alone mourned.
translations.idiomatic: His colleagues offered congratulations; only Zhao Ping came to condole.

sentences.zh: 召平者，故秦東陵侯。

translations.literal: Zhao Ping had been the Marquis of Dongling of Qin.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Ping was the former Marquis of Dongling under Qin.

sentences.zh: 秦破，為布衣，貧，種瓜長安城東，瓜美，故世謂「東陵瓜」，從召平始也。

translations.literal: When Qin fell he became a commoner; poor, he planted melons east of Chang’an; the melons were fine, so the world calls them “Dongling melons,” beginning from Zhao Ping.
translations.idiomatic: After Qin’s fall he lived as a commoner in poverty, growing melons east of Chang’an so fine that people still speak of “Dongling melons,” after him.

sentences.zh: 平謂何曰：「禍自此始矣。

translations.literal: Ping said to He, “From here your calamity begins.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Ping told Xiao He, “Your troubles start here.

sentences.zh: 上暴露於外，而君守於內，非被矢石之難，而益君封置衛者，以今者淮陰新反於中，有疑君心。

translations.literal: The Sovereign is exposed in the field while you guard within; you have not endured the hardship of arrows and stones, yet he increases your fief and posts guards—because the Marquis of Huaiyin has lately rebelled within, he harbors doubts about you.
translations.idiomatic: The Sovereign braves the frontier while you sit in the capital, never dodging arrows or stones, yet he enlarges your fief and stations guards about you—because the Marquis of Huaiyin has just risen inside the passes, he no longer trusts you.

sentences.zh: 夫置衛衛君，非以寵君也。

translations.literal: Posting guards to guard the lord is not to honor the lord.”
translations.idiomatic: Those guards are not posted for your glory.”

sentences.zh: 願君讓封勿受，悉以家私財佐軍。」

translations.literal: I wish you to decline the added fief and not accept it, and devote all your private wealth to aid the army.”
translations.idiomatic: Refuse the extra enfeoffment and pour your private fortune into the war effort instead.”

sentences.zh: 何從其計，上說。

translations.literal: He followed his plan, and the Sovereign was pleased.
translations.idiomatic: Xiao He obeyed, and the Sovereign was pleased.

sentences.zh: 其秋，黥布反，上自將擊之，數使使問相國何為。

translations.literal: That autumn Qing Bu rebelled; the Sovereign personally led the army to strike him and repeatedly sent messengers to ask what the minister of state He was doing.
translations.idiomatic: That autumn Qing Bu rebelled; the Sovereign took the field against him and kept sending messengers to ask what Minister of State Xiao He was doing.

sentences.zh: 曰：「為上在軍，拊循勉百姓，悉所有佐軍，如陳豨時。」

translations.literal: [He] said, “Because the Sovereign is in the army, I console and encourage the common people and devote all I have to aid the army, as at the time of Chen Xi.”
translations.idiomatic: Xiao He replied that, with the Sovereign away, he was comforting the people and spending his own wealth on the army, just as during Chen Xi’s revolt.

sentences.zh: 客又說何曰：「君滅族不久矣。

translations.literal: A guest again said to He, “Your clan will soon be exterminated.
translations.idiomatic: Another adviser warned him, “You are courting extermination for your whole line.

sentences.zh: 夫君位為相國，功第一，不可復加。

translations.literal: My lord, your position is minister of state and your achievement is first; nothing further can be added.
translations.idiomatic: You are minister of state and already first in merit—there is nowhere higher to climb.

sentences.zh: 然君初入關，本得百姓心，十餘年矣。

translations.literal: Yet when my lord first entered the Pass, you fundamentally won the hearts of the common people, and it has been more than ten years.
translations.idiomatic: Yet from the day you entered the passes you won the people’s hearts, and more than ten years have passed.

sentences.zh: 皆附君，尚復孳孳得民和。

translations.literal: All cling to you, and you still diligently win the harmony of the people.
translations.idiomatic: They still look to you, and you keep burnishing your popularity.

sentences.zh: 上所謂數問君，畏君傾動關中。

translations.literal: What the Sovereign calls repeatedly asking after you is fearing that you will shake Guanzhong.
translations.idiomatic: Each time he asks after you, he is gauging whether you might sway Guanzhong against him.

sentences.zh: 今君胡不多買田地，賤貰貣以自汙？

translations.literal: Why does my lord not buy up land in great quantity and borrow at ruinous rates to defile himself?
translations.idiomatic: Buy land cheap, lend at harsh interest—dirty your own name.

sentences.zh: 上心必安。」

translations.literal: Then the Sovereign’s mind will be at ease.”
translations.idiomatic: Only then will he feel secure.”

sentences.zh: 於是何從其計，上乃大說。

translations.literal: Thereupon He followed his plan, and the Sovereign was greatly pleased.
translations.idiomatic: Xiao He followed the advice, and the Sovereign was greatly relieved.

sentences.zh: 上罷布軍歸，民道遮行，上書言相國彊賤買民田宅數千人。

translations.literal: When the Sovereign dismissed the army after Qing Bu and returned, the people along the road blocked his progress and submitted memorials saying the minister of state He forcibly bought the fields and houses of the common people at low price, several thousand persons.
translations.idiomatic: On the way home from the Qing Bu campaign, commoners mobbed the road with petitions accusing Minister of State Xiao He of bullying thousands of families into selling land and houses for a pittance.

sentences.zh: 上至，何謁。

translations.literal: When the Sovereign arrived, He paid his respects.
translations.idiomatic: When the Sovereign reached the capital, Xiao He came to audience.

sentences.zh: 上笑曰：「今相國乃利民！」

translations.literal: The Sovereign laughed and said, “Now the minister of state profits the people!”
translations.idiomatic: The Sovereign laughed and said, “So my minister of state has been fleecing the people for their own good!”

sentences.zh: 民所上書皆以與何，曰：「君自謝民。」

translations.literal: The memorials the people had submitted he all gave to He, saying, “You yourself apologize to the people.”
translations.idiomatic: He handed Xiao He the sheaf of petitions and told him, “You may apologize to them yourself.”

sentences.zh: 後何為民請曰：「長安地骥，上林中多空地，棄，願令民得入田，毋收稿為獸食。」

translations.literal: Later He made request on behalf of the people, saying, “The land of Chang’an is cramped; within the Shanglin Park there is much empty ground lying waste; I wish to allow the people to enter and farm it, and not take the straw so that it may be food for the beasts.”
translations.idiomatic: Later Xiao He pleaded for the people: “Chang’an is crowded, while the Shanglin hunting park lies largely empty; let commoners till those fields and leave the stubble for the imperial herds.”

sentences.zh: 上大怒曰：「相國多受賈人財物，為請吾苑！」

translations.literal: The Sovereign was greatly angry and said, “The minister of state has received much wealth from merchants and on their behalf requests my park!”
translations.idiomatic: The Sovereign exploded: “The minister of state has been bribed by merchants to beg for my hunting preserve!”

sentences.zh: 乃下何廷尉，械繫之。

translations.literal: Thereupon he handed He to the commandant of the court, shackling and binding him.
translations.idiomatic: He had Xiao He clapped in fetters and handed to the commandant of justice.

sentences.zh: 數日，王衛尉侍，前問曰：「相國胡大罪，陛下繫之暴也？」

translations.literal: After several days, Commandant of the Guards Wang, while in attendance, stepped forward and asked, “What great crime has the minister of state committed, that Your Majesty has bound him so harshly?”
translations.idiomatic: A few days later Commandant Wang of the palace guard, on duty at court, stepped forward: “What grave offense could warrant clapping the minister of state in irons like a common criminal?”

sentences.zh: 上曰：「吾聞李斯相秦皇帝，有善歸主，有惡自予。

translations.literal: The Sovereign said, “I have heard that when Li Si served as chancellor to the First Emperor of Qin, whatever was good he attributed to his lord; whatever was bad he took on himself.
translations.idiomatic: The Sovereign said, “They say Li Si, as Qin’s chancellor, credited the throne with every success and himself with every failure;

sentences.zh: 今相國多受賈豎金，為請吾苑，以自媚於民。

translations.literal: Now the minister of state has accepted much gold from huckster merchants, and on their behalf has requested my park, thereby to curry favor with the people for himself.”
translations.idiomatic: “Now my minister of state is said to have taken merchants’ gold to beg my hunting park—plainly to buy popularity.”

sentences.zh: 故繫治之。」

translations.literal: Therefore I bound and tried him.”
translations.idiomatic: “So I had him arrested.”

sentences.zh: 王衛尉曰：「夫職事苟有便於民而請之，真宰相事也。

translations.literal: Wang said, “In official duty, if something benefits the people and one requests it—that is truly a chancellor’s business.
translations.idiomatic: Wang answered, “When an officer petitions for anything that helps the people, that is precisely what a chief minister should do.”

sentences.zh: 陛下柰何乃疑相國受賈人錢乎！

translations.literal: How can Your Majesty suspect the minister of state of taking merchants’ money!
translations.idiomatic: How can you imagine he lined his pockets from shopkeepers?”

sentences.zh: 且陛下距楚數歲，陳豨、黥布反時，陛下自將往，當是時相國守關中，關中搖足則關西非陛下有也。

translations.literal: Moreover, for several years Your Majesty resisted Chu; when Chen Xi and Qing Bu rebelled, Your Majesty personally led the army thither; at that time the minister of state guarded Guanzhong—had Guanzhong so much as shifted a foot, the land west of the Pass would no longer have been Yours.”
translations.idiomatic: “For years you faced Chu in the field; when Chen Xi and Qing Bu rose, you marched yourself—while the minister of state held Guanzhong. Had that heartland wavered, nothing west of the passes would still be yours.”

sentences.zh: 相國不以此時為利，乃利賈人之金乎？

translations.literal: Would the minister of state not have profited then, and instead profit from merchants’ gold?
translations.idiomatic: If he meant to profit from crisis, he had his chance then—would he wait to scrape cash from peddlers?”

sentences.zh: 且秦以不聞其過亡天下，夫李斯之分過，又何足法哉！

translations.literal: Moreover Qin lost the empire because it would not hear of its faults; as for Li Si’s passing blame to others—how is that worth taking as a model!
translations.idiomatic: Qin fell because it never heard its own faults; Li Si’s habit of shifting blame is hardly a pattern to follow.”

sentences.zh: 陛下何疑宰相之淺也！」

translations.literal: How shallowly Your Majesty doubts your chancellor!”
translations.idiomatic: You misjudge your chancellor badly.”

sentences.zh: 上不懌。

translations.literal: The Sovereign was displeased.
translations.idiomatic: The Sovereign’s face darkened.

sentences.zh: 是日，使使持節赦出何。

translations.literal: That day he sent an envoy bearing credentials to pardon and release He.
translations.idiomatic: That same day he sent an envoy with imperial credentials to free Xiao He.

sentences.zh: 何年老，素恭謹，徒跣入謝。

translations.literal: He being old, always respectful and careful, went in barefoot to apologize.
translations.idiomatic: Old and habitually deferential, Xiao He came barefoot to beg forgiveness.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「相國休矣！

translations.literal: The Sovereign said, “Minister of state, enough!
translations.idiomatic: “Enough, minister of state,” said the Sovereign.

sentences.zh: 相國為民請吾苑不許，我不過為桀紂主，而相國為賢相。

translations.literal: The minister of state requested my park for the people and I would not grant it; I would have been no more than a Jie or Zhou of a lord, while the minister of state would have been the worthy chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: “Had I refused your plea for the people, I would have ranked with Jie and Zhou, while you stood as the good minister.”

sentences.zh: 吾故繫相國，欲令百姓聞吾過。」

translations.literal: I deliberately bound the minister of state because I wished the common people to hear of my fault.”
translations.idiomatic: “I jailed you so the realm would hear of my mistake.”

sentences.zh: 高祖崩，何事惠帝。

translations.literal: When Gaozu died, He served Emperor Hui.
translations.idiomatic: After Gaozu’s death Xiao He served Emperor Hui.

sentences.zh: 何病，上親自臨視何疾，因問曰：「君即百歲後，誰可代君？」

translations.literal: He fell ill; the Sovereign personally visited to observe his illness and then asked, “When you reach a hundred years, who may replace you?”
translations.idiomatic: When Xiao He fell ill, the Sovereign came to his bedside and asked, “When you are gone, who can take your place?”

sentences.zh: 對曰：「知臣莫如主。」

translations.literal: He replied, “None knows a minister like his lord.”
translations.idiomatic: He answered, “No one knows his servant better than his master.”

sentences.zh: 帝曰：「曹參何如？」

translations.literal: The Emperor said, “How about Cao Shen?”
translations.idiomatic: “What of Cao Shen?” asked the Emperor.

sentences.zh: 何頓首曰：「帝得之矣。

translations.literal: He knocked his head and said, “The Emperor has found the right man.
translations.idiomatic: Xiao He kowtowed. “Your Majesty has chosen well.”

sentences.zh: 何死不恨矣！」

translations.literal: He dies without regret!”
translations.idiomatic: “I may die content.”

sentences.zh: 何買田宅必居窮辟處，為家不治垣屋。

translations.literal: In buying fields and houses He always chose those in poor out-of-the-way places, and for his household did not repair the walls and roofs.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever Xiao He bought land or houses, he picked poor, unfashionable neighborhoods and left his walls and roofs unrenovated.

sentences.zh: 曰：「令後世賢，師吾儉；

translations.literal: He said, “If later generations are worthy, let them imitate my frugality;
translations.idiomatic: He explained, “If my heirs prove worthy, they may learn thrift from this;

sentences.zh: 不賢，毋為勢家所奪。」

translations.literal: if not worthy, let them not be seized from by powerful families.”
translations.idiomatic: if they prove worthless, powerful families will not covet these holdings.”

sentences.zh: 孝惠二年，何薨，諡曰文終侯。

translations.literal: In the second year of Emperor Hui’s reign, He died and was posthumously titled Marquis Wenzhong.
translations.idiomatic: In the second year of Emperor Hui’s reign Xiao He died; his posthumous title was Marquis Wenzhong.

sentences.zh: 子祿嗣，薨，無子。

translations.literal: His son Lu succeeded, died, and had no son.
translations.idiomatic: His son Lu inherited the title, then died without issue.

sentences.zh: 高后乃封何夫人同為酇侯，小子延為筑陽侯。

translations.literal: Empress Gao therefore enfeoffed He’s wife Tong as Marquis of Zan, and the younger son Yan as Marquis of Zhuyang.
translations.idiomatic: Empress Lü thereupon made Xiao He’s widow Tong Marquis of Zan and his younger son Yan Marquis of Zhuyang.

sentences.zh: 孝文元年，罷同，更封延為酇侯。

translations.literal: In the first year of Emperor Wen’s reign, Tong was removed and Yan was transferred to be Marquis of Zan.
translations.idiomatic: In Emperor Wen’s first year Tong was stripped of her fief and Yan was reassigned as Marquis of Zan.

sentences.zh: 薨，子遺嗣。

translations.literal: He died; his son Yi succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: When Yan died, his son Yi succeeded.

sentences.zh: 薨，無子。

translations.literal: He died; there was no son.
translations.idiomatic: Yi died childless.

sentences.zh: 文帝復以遺弟則嗣，有罪免。

translations.literal: Emperor Wen again had Yi's younger brother Ze succeed; for a crime he was removed.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wen then named Yi's younger brother Ze heir; Ze was later dismissed for an offense.

sentences.zh: 景帝二年，制詔御史：「故相國蕭何，高皇帝大功臣，所與為天下也。

translations.literal: In the second year of Emperor Jing’s reign, an edict was issued to the imperial clerk: “The former minister of state Xiao He was a great meritorious minister of the August Emperor, with whom he took possession of the empire.
translations.idiomatic: In Emperor Jing’s second year the throne ordered the censor: “Xiao He, late minister of state, was a pillar of the August Emperor’s rise; with him the founder won the realm.”

sentences.zh: 今其祀絕，朕甚憐之。

translations.literal: Now his sacrifices are cut off; We are deeply moved.
translations.idiomatic: “His line now lacks heirs to tend his shrine, and We grieve for it.”

sentences.zh: 其以武陽縣戶二千封何孫嘉為列侯。」

translations.literal: We therefore enfeoff his grandson Jia as a ranked marquis with two thousand households in Wuyang county.”
translations.idiomatic: “We therefore create his grandson Jia a ranked marquis with two thousand households at Wuyang.”

sentences.zh: 嘉，則弟也。

translations.literal: Jia was Ze’s younger brother.
translations.idiomatic: Jia was Ze’s younger brother.

sentences.zh: 薨，子勝嗣，後有罪免。

translations.literal: He died; his son Sheng succeeded, and later for a crime was removed.
translations.idiomatic: Jia died; his son Sheng inherited, then lost the title for a crime.

sentences.zh: 武帝元狩中，復下詔御史：「以酇戶二千四百封何曾孫慶為酇侯，布告天下，令明知朕報蕭相國德也。」

translations.literal: In the Yuanshou era of Emperor Wu, an edict was again issued to the clerk: “With twenty-four hundred households at Zan, We enfeoff Xiao He’s great-grandson Qing as Marquis of Zan, and proclaim it to the empire so all plainly know We repay the virtue of Minister Xiao.”
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Wu, in the Yuanshou period, another edict ran: “With twenty-four hundred households at Zan We enfeoff Xiao He’s great-grandson Qing as Marquis of Zan and publish this so the world knows We honor the house of Minister Xiao.”

sentences.zh: 慶，則子也。

translations.literal: Qing was Ze’s son.
translations.idiomatic: Qing was Ze’s son.

sentences.zh: 薨，子壽成嗣，坐為太常儀牲瘦免。

translations.literal: He died; his son Shoucheng succeeded, and was removed for the offense that, as grand master of ceremonies, his ritual victims were emaciated.
translations.idiomatic: Qing died; Shoucheng succeeded but was dismissed because, as grand master of ceremonies, his sacrificial animals were judged too lean.

sentences.zh: 宣帝時，詔丞相御史求問蕭相國後在者，得玄孫建世等十二人，復下詔以酇戶二千封建世為酇侯。

translations.literal: In Emperor Xuan’s time an edict ordered the chancellor and imperial clerk to seek survivors among Minister Xiao’s posterity; they found twelve men including great-great-grandson Jianshi, and an edict was issued again enfeoffing Jianshi as Marquis of Zan with two thousand households at Zan.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Xuan ordered the chancellor and censor to locate any surviving descendants of Minister Xiao; twelve men were found, among them Jianshi, whom the court again enfeoffed at Zan with two thousand households.

sentences.zh: 傳子至孫獲，坐使奴殺人減死論。

translations.literal: It passed to a son and then to grandson Huo, who was sentenced to reduced death for having slaves commit murder.
translations.idiomatic: The title passed to a son and then to grandson Huo, who was spared execution but condemned for letting a slave kill a man.

sentences.zh: 成帝時，復封何玄孫之子南讀長喜為酇侯。

translations.literal: In Emperor Cheng’s time Xiao He’s collateral descendant’s son Xi, chief of Nandu, was again enfeoffed as Marquis of Zan.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Cheng the court once more named Xi—son of a collateral descendant of Xiao He and chief of Nandu—Marquis of Zan.

sentences.zh: 傳子至曾孫，王莽敗乃絕。

translations.literal: It passed to a son down to great-great-grandson, and only with Wang Mang’s fall did it end.
translations.idiomatic: The line ran through sons to a great-great-grandson and finally died out when Wang Mang fell.

sentences.zh: 曹參，沛人也。

translations.literal: Cao Shen was a man of Pei.
translations.idiomatic: Cao Shen came from Pei.

sentences.zh: 秦時為獄掾，而蕭何為主吏，居縣為豪吏矣。

translations.literal: Under Qin he was clerk of the jail, while Xiao He was chief clerk; in the county they were the leading clerks.
translations.idiomatic: Under the Qin he served as jail clerk while Xiao He was chief clerk; together they ranked as the county’s foremost officers.

sentences.zh: 高祖為沛公也，參以中涓從。

translations.literal: When Gaozu became Duke of Pei, Shen followed as palace attendant within.
translations.idiomatic: When Liu Bang became Duke of Pei, Cao Shen joined him as an inner attendant.

sentences.zh: 擊胡陵、方與，攻秦監公軍，大破之。

translations.literal: He struck Huling and Fangyu, attacked the army of the Qin inspector, and smashed it.
translations.idiomatic: He fought at Huling and Fangyu, routed the army of Qin’s local inspector, and broke it utterly.

sentences.zh: 東下薛，擊泗水守軍薛郭西。

translations.literal: He went east down to Xue, struck the army of the Sishui defender west of Xue’s outer wall.
translations.idiomatic: He marched east to Xue and attacked the Sishui governor’s force west of the city wall.

sentences.zh: 復攻胡陵，取之。

translations.literal: Again he attacked Huling and took it.
translations.idiomatic: He attacked Huling again and captured it.

sentences.zh: 徙守方與。

translations.literal: He was moved to guard Fangyu.
translations.idiomatic: He was reassigned to hold Fangyu.

sentences.zh: 方與反為魏，擊之。

translations.literal: Fangyu turned to Wei; he attacked it.
translations.idiomatic: When Fangyu went over to Wei, he struck it back.

sentences.zh: 豐反為魏，攻之。

translations.literal: Feng turned to Wei; he attacked it.
translations.idiomatic: When Feng declared for Wei, he attacked it.

sentences.zh: 賜爵七大夫。

translations.literal: He was granted the rank of seventh grandee.
translations.idiomatic: He was awarded the seventh rank of grandee.

sentences.zh: 北擊司馬欣軍碭東，取狐父、祁善置。

translations.literal: He struck north against Sima Xin’s army east of Dang, taking Hufu and the Qishan relay station.
translations.idiomatic: He drove north against Sima Xin’s host east of Dang, seizing Hufu and the Qishan courier post.

sentences.zh: 又攻下邑以西，至虞，擊秦將章邯車騎。

translations.literal: Again he attacked west of Xiayi to Yu, striking Qin general Zhang Han’s chariots and cavalry.
translations.idiomatic: He pushed west from Xiayi to Yu and engaged the chariotry and horse of Qin’s Zhang Han.

sentences.zh: 攻轅戚及亢父，先登。

translations.literal: He attacked Yuancheng and Kangfu, being first to mount the wall.
translations.idiomatic: At Yuancheng and Kangfu he led the first assault over the walls.

sentences.zh: 遷為五大夫。

translations.literal: He was promoted to fifth grandee.
translations.idiomatic: He was raised to fifth grandee.

sentences.zh: 北救東阿，擊章邯軍，陷陳，追至濮陽。

translations.literal: He rescued Dong’e to the north, struck Zhang Han’s army, broke the formation, and pursued to Puyang.
translations.idiomatic: He relieved Dong’e in the north, shattered Zhang Han’s battle line, and chased him to Puyang.

sentences.zh: 攻定陶，取臨濟。

translations.literal: He attacked Dingtao and took Linji.
translations.idiomatic: He took Dingtao and captured Linji.

sentences.zh: 南救雍丘，擊李由軍，破之，殺李由，虜秦候一人。

translations.literal: He rescued Yongqiu to the south, struck Li You’s army, destroyed it, killed Li You, and captured one Qin noble (hou).
translations.idiomatic: Marching south to Yongqiu he crushed Li You’s army, slew Li You, and took a Qin marquis prisoner.

sentences.zh: 章邯破殺項梁也，沛公與項羽引兵而東。

translations.literal: When Zhang Han defeated and killed Xiang Liang, the Duke of Pei with Xiang Yu led the army eastward.
translations.idiomatic: After Zhang Han destroyed Xiang Liang, the Duke of Pei and Xiang Yu withdrew eastward.

sentences.zh: 楚懷王以沛公為碭郡長，將碭郡兵。

translations.literal: King Huai of Chu appointed the Duke of Pei as governor of Dang commandery and put him in command of Dang’s troops.
translations.idiomatic: King Huai of Chu named the Duke of Pei governor of Dang and gave him command of its army.

sentences.zh: 於是乃封參執帛，號曰建成君。

translations.literal: Thereupon they enfeoffed Shen with the ceremonial tablet, titled Lord Jiancheng.
translations.idiomatic: Cao Shen was then invested with the silk tablet and styled Lord Jiancheng.

sentences.zh: 遷為戚公，屬碭郡。

translations.literal: He was promoted to lord of Qi, subordinate to Dang commandery.
translations.idiomatic: He was promoted to lord of Qi township under Dang commandery.

sentences.zh: 其後從攻東郡尉軍，破之成武南。

translations.literal: Afterward he followed in attacking the army of the eastern commandery commandant, destroying it south of Chengwu.
translations.idiomatic: Later he joined the strike on the eastern commandery’s commandant and routed him south of Chengwu.

sentences.zh: 擊王離軍成陽南，又攻杠里，大破之。

translations.literal: He struck Wang Li’s army south of Chengyang, again attacked Gangli, and smashed it.
translations.idiomatic: He hit Wang Li south of Chengyang, then stormed Gangli and broke it.

sentences.zh: 追北，西至開封，擊趙賁軍，破之，圍趙賁開封城中。

translations.literal: Pursuing the routed, west to Kaifeng, he struck Zhao Ben’s army, destroyed it, and besieged Zhao Ben in Kaifeng city.
translations.idiomatic: He chased the fugitives west to Kaifeng, defeated Zhao Ben, and penned him inside the city.

sentences.zh: 西擊秦將楊熊軍於曲遇，破之，虜秦司馬及御史各一人。

translations.literal: West he struck Qin general Yang Xiong’s army at Quyu, destroyed it, and captured one Qin major and one imperial clerk each.
translations.idiomatic: West of the lines he smashed Yang Xiong’s Qin army at Quyu and took one major and one censor prisoner.

sentences.zh: 遷為執珪。

translations.literal: He was promoted to jade-tablet holder.
translations.idiomatic: He was advanced to the rank of jade sceptre.

sentences.zh: 從西攻陽武，下轘轅、緱氏，絕河津。

translations.literal: Following west he attacked Yangwu, reduced Huanyuan and Zoushi, and cut the river ford.
translations.idiomatic: He followed the advance on Yangwu, seized Huanyuan and Zoushi, and severed the Yellow River crossing.

sentences.zh: 擊趙賁軍尸北，破之。

translations.literal: He struck Zhao Ben’s army north of Shi, destroying it.
translations.idiomatic: He defeated Zhao Ben’s force north of Shi.

sentences.zh: 從南攻犨，與南陽守齮戰陽城郭東，陷陳，取宛，虜齮，定南陽郡。

translations.literal: Following south he attacked Chou, fought Nanyang defender Yi east of Yangcheng’s wall, broke the formation, took Wan, captured Yi, and pacified Nanyang commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Turning south he fought the Nanyang governor Yi east of Yangcheng, broke his line, captured Wan and Yi himself, and secured the commandery.

sentences.zh: 從西攻武關、嶢關，取之。

translations.literal: Following west he attacked Wu Pass and Yao Pass and took them.
translations.idiomatic: He pressed west through Wu Pass and Yao Pass and carried both.

sentences.zh: 前攻秦軍藍田南，又夜擊其北軍，大破之，遂至咸陽，破秦。

translations.literal: Advancing he attacked the Qin army south of Lantian, again by night struck their northern camp, smashed it, then reached Xianyang and destroyed Qin.
translations.idiomatic: South of Lantian he routed the Qin host, then by night smashed their northern division, marched into Xianyang, and finished Qin.

sentences.zh: 項羽至，以沛公為漢王。

translations.literal: When Xiang Yu arrived he made the Duke of Pei King of Han.
translations.idiomatic: When Xiang Yu came up he named the Duke of Pei King of Han.

sentences.zh: 漢王封參為建成侯。

translations.literal: The King of Han enfeoffed Shen as Marquis of Jiancheng.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Han enfeoffed Cao Shen as Marquis of Jiancheng.

sentences.zh: 從至漢中，遷為將軍。

translations.literal: He followed to Hanzhong and was promoted to general.
translations.idiomatic: He accompanied the court to Hanzhong and was promoted general.

sentences.zh: 從還定三秦，攻下辨、故道、雍、斄。

translations.literal: He followed in the return to settle the Three Qin, attacking Xiabian, Gudao, Yong, and Tai.
translations.idiomatic: On the campaign to reconquer the Three Qin he took Xiabian, Gudao, Yong, and Tai.

sentences.zh: 擊章平軍於好畤南，破之，圍好畤，取壤鄉。

translations.literal: He struck Zhang Ping’s army south of Haozhi, destroyed it, besieged Haozhi, and took Xiang township.
translations.idiomatic: South of Haozhi he broke Zhang Ping, besieged the town, and seized Xiang township.

sentences.zh: 擊三秦軍壤東及高櫟，破之。

translations.literal: He struck the Three Qin armies east of Xiang and at Gaoli, destroying them.
translations.idiomatic: He smashed Three Qin forces east of Xiang and at Gaoli.

sentences.zh: 復圍章平，平出好畤走。

translations.literal: Again he besieged Zhang Ping; Ping fled out of Haozhi.
translations.idiomatic: He besieged Zhang Ping again until Zhang Ping broke out of Haozhi and fled.

sentences.zh: 因擊趙賁、內史保軍，破之。

translations.literal: Then he struck Zhao Ben and Neishi Bao’s army, destroying it.
translations.idiomatic: He followed up by crushing Zhao Ben and the palace commandant Bao.

sentences.zh: 東取咸陽，更名曰新城。

translations.literal: East he took Xianyang, renaming it New City.
translations.idiomatic: He seized Xianyang to the east and renamed it New City.

sentences.zh: 參將兵守景陵二十三日，三秦使章平等攻參，參出擊，大破之。

translations.literal: Shen led troops and guarded Jingling twenty-three days; the Three Qin sent Zhang Ping and others to attack Shen; Shen sallied forth and smashed them.
translations.idiomatic: Cao Shen held Jingling for twenty-three days; when Three Qin generals led by Zhang Ping attacked, he sallied and shattered them.

sentences.zh: 賜食邑於寧秦。

translations.literal: He was granted a fief at Ningqin.
translations.idiomatic: He received a food fief at Ningqin.

sentences.zh: 以將軍引兵圍章邯廢丘；

translations.literal: As general he led troops to besiege Zhang Han at Feiqiu;
translations.idiomatic: As general he led the siege that penned Zhang Han in Feiqiu.

sentences.zh: 以中尉從漢王出臨晉關。

translations.literal: as colonel of the guard he followed the King of Han out through Linjin Pass.
translations.idiomatic: as colonel of the guard he escorted the King of Han through Linjin Pass.

sentences.zh: 至河內，下脩武，度圍津，東擊龍且、項佗定陶，破之。

translations.literal: Reaching Henei, he took Xiuwu, crossed Weijin ford, struck east at Long Ju and Xiang Tuo at Dingtao, and destroyed them.
translations.idiomatic: In Henei he reduced Xiuwu, crossed the ford at Weijin, and eastward defeated Long Ju and Xiang Tuo at Dingtao.

sentences.zh: 東取碭、蕭、彭城。

translations.literal: East he took Dang, Xiao, and Pengcheng.
translations.idiomatic: He drove east to take Dang, Xiao, and Pengcheng.

sentences.zh: 擊項籍軍，漢軍大敗走。

translations.literal: He struck Xiang Yu’s army; the Han army was badly defeated and fled.
translations.idiomatic: There he collided with Xiang Yu’s host and the Han army was routed.

sentences.zh: 參以中尉圍取雍丘。

translations.literal: Shen as colonel of the guard besieged and took Yongqiu.
translations.idiomatic: As colonel of the guard Cao Shen besieged and took Yongqiu.

sentences.zh: 王武反於外黃，程處反於燕，往擊，盡破之。

translations.literal: Wang Wu rebelled at Waihuang and Cheng Chu at Yan; he went and struck, destroying both.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Wu rose at Waihuang and Cheng Chu at Yan; Cao Shen marched against them and crushed both revolts.

sentences.zh: 柱天侯反於衍氏，進破取衍氏。

translations.literal: The Pillar-of-Heaven marquis rebelled at Yanshi; he advanced, destroyed, and took Yanshi.
translations.idiomatic: When the Pillar-of-Heaven marquis mutinied at Yanshi, Cao Shen advanced, broke him, and occupied the town.

sentences.zh: 擊羽嬰於昆陽，追至葉。

translations.literal: He struck Yu Ying at Kunyang and pursued to Ye.
translations.idiomatic: He fought Yu Ying at Kunyang and chased him to Ye.

sentences.zh: 還攻武彊，因至滎陽。

translations.literal: Turning back he attacked Wuqiang, then arrived at Xingyang.
translations.idiomatic: On the withdrawal he stormed Wuqiang and then fell back on Xingyang.

sentences.zh: 參自漢中為將軍中尉，從擊諸侯，及項王敗，還至滎陽。

translations.literal: Shen from Hanzhong served as general and colonel of the guard, followed in striking the feudal lords, and when the King of Xiang was defeated, returned to Xingyang.
translations.idiomatic: From Hanzhong onward Cao Shen held the ranks of general and colonel of the guard, campaigned against the lords, and when Xiang Yu fell he was back at Xingyang.

sentences.zh: 漢二年，拜為假左丞相，入屯兵關中。

translations.literal: In the second year of Han he was appointed acting left chancellor and entered to station troops in Guanzhong.
translations.idiomatic: In the second Han year he was named acting left chancellor and sent into Guanzhong to garrison troops.

sentences.zh: 月餘，魏王豹反，以假丞相別與韓信東攻魏將孫速東張，大破之。

translations.literal: After a month more the King of Wei Bao rebelled; as acting chancellor in a separate column with Han Xin he struck east against Wei general Sun Su at Dongzhang, smashing him.
translations.idiomatic: A month later King Bao of Wei rebelled; Cao Shen, still acting chancellor, joined Han Xin in a separate column, struck Wei general Sun Su at Dongzhang, and shattered him.

sentences.zh: 因攻安邑，得魏將王襄。

translations.literal: Then he attacked Anyi and captured Wei general Wang Xiang.
translations.idiomatic: He pressed on to Anyi and took Wei general Wang Xiang prisoner.

sentences.zh: 擊魏王於曲陽，追至東垣，生獲魏王豹。

translations.literal: He struck the King of Wei at Quyang, pursued to Dongyuan, and captured alive the King of Wei, Bao.
translations.idiomatic: At Quyang he engaged King Bao of Wei, chased him to Dongyuan, and took him alive.

sentences.zh: 取平陽，得豹母妻子，盡定魏地，凡五十二縣。

translations.literal: He took Pingyang, obtained Bao’s mother, wife, and children, wholly pacified Wei territory—in all fifty-two counties.
translations.idiomatic: He seized Pingyang, rounded up Bao’s family, and subdued all of Wei, fifty-two counties in total.

sentences.zh: 賜食邑平陽。

translations.literal: He was granted a food fief at Pingyang.
translations.idiomatic: The court awarded him a revenue fief at Pingyang.

sentences.zh: 因從韓信擊趙相國夏說軍於鄔東，大破之，斬夏說。

translations.literal: Following, he joined Han Xin in striking Zhao chancellor Xia Shuo’s army east of Wu, smashed it, and beheaded Xia Shuo.
translations.idiomatic: He then followed Han Xin, routed Zhao’s chancellor Xia Shuo east of Wu, and executed Xia Shuo.

sentences.zh: 韓信與故常山王張耳引兵下井陘，擊成安君陳餘，而令參還圍趙別將戚公於鄔城中。

translations.literal: Han Xin with the former King of Changshan Zhang Er led troops down Jingxing to strike Lord Cheng’an Chen Yu, and ordered Shen in return to besiege Zhao’s detached general Lord Qi in Wu city.
translations.idiomatic: While Han Xin and Zhang Er drove through Jingxing against Chen Yu, Cao Shen was sent back to bottle up Zhao’s general Qi in Wu.

sentences.zh: 戚公出走，追斬之。

translations.literal: Lord Qi fled out; [Shen] pursued and beheaded him.
translations.idiomatic: When Qi broke out and ran, Cao Shen overtook him and cut him down.

sentences.zh: 乃引兵詣漢王在所。

translations.literal: Then he led troops to where the King of Han was.
translations.idiomatic: He then marched to join the King of Han.

sentences.zh: 韓信已破趙，為相國，東擊齊，參以左丞相屬焉。

translations.literal: Han Xin having destroyed Zhao became chancellor of state and struck east at Qi; Shen was attached to him as left chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: After Han Xin crushed Zhao he was named chancellor of state and turned east on Qi; Cao Shen served under him as left chancellor.

sentences.zh: 攻破齊歷下軍，遂取臨淄。

translations.literal: He broke and overcame Qi’s army at Lixia, then took Linzi.
translations.idiomatic: He shattered the Qi host at Lixia and captured the capital Linzi.

sentences.zh: 還定濟北郡，收著、漯陰、平原、鬲、盧。

translations.literal: Returning he pacified Jibei commandery, taking Zhu, Luoyin, Pingyuan, Ge, and Lu.
translations.idiomatic: He secured Jibei on the way back, recovering Zhu, Luoyin, Pingyuan, Ge, and Lu.

sentences.zh: 已而從韓信擊龍且軍於上假密，大破之，斬龍且，虜亞將周蘭。

translations.literal: Subsequently he followed Han Xin in striking Long Ju’s army at Shangjiami, smashed it, beheaded Long Ju, and captured deputy general Zhou Lan.
translations.idiomatic: He next followed Han Xin against Long Ju at Shangjiami, broke his army, slew Long Ju, and took Zhou Lan, his second-in-command, prisoner.

sentences.zh: 定齊郡，凡得七十縣。

translations.literal: He pacified the commanderies of Qi, in all obtaining seventy counties.
translations.idiomatic: He brought the whole of Qi under control—seventy counties.

sentences.zh: 得故齊王田廣相田光，其守相許章，及故將軍田既。

translations.literal: He captured Tian Guang, chancellor to the former King of Qi Tian Kuang, Xu Zhang the guardian chancellor, and the former general Tian Ji.
translations.idiomatic: He seized Tian Guang, who had been chancellor under the late King of Qi, Xu Zhang who held the seal as acting governor, and the former general Tian Ji.

sentences.zh: 韓信立為齊王，引兵東詣陳，與漢王共破項羽，而參留平齊未服者。

translations.literal: Han Xin being established as King of Qi led troops east to Chen, with the King of Han together destroying Xiang Yu, while Shen remained to pacify those of Qi not yet submitted.
translations.idiomatic: After Han Xin was made King of Qi he marched east to Chen to help the King of Han destroy Xiang Yu, while Cao Shen stayed to mop up Qi’s holdouts.

sentences.zh: 漢王即皇帝位，韓信徙為楚王。

translations.literal: The King of Han assumed the imperial position; Han Xin was transferred to be King of Chu.
translations.idiomatic: When Liu Bang took the throne, Han Xin was moved to the kingship of Chu.

sentences.zh: 參歸相印焉。

translations.literal: Shen returned the chancellor’s seal.
translations.idiomatic: Cao Shen handed back his chancellor’s seal.

sentences.zh: 高祖以長子肥為齊王，而以參為相國。

translations.literal: Gaozu appointed his eldest son Fei King of Qi, and made Shen minister of state.
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu made his eldest son Liu Fei king of Qi and named Cao Shen minister of state to him.

sentences.zh: 高祖六年，與諸侯剖符，賜參爵列侯，食邑平陽萬六百三十戶，世世勿絕。

translations.literal: In the sixth year of Gaozu, splitting tallies with the feudal lords, he granted Shen the rank of ranked marquis with an estate at Pingyang of ten thousand six hundred thirty households, generation after generation not to be cut off.
translations.idiomatic: In Gaozu’s sixth year he split the tally with the lords and enfeoffed Cao Shen at Pingyang with 10,630 households in perpetuity.

sentences.zh: 參以齊相國擊陳豨將張春，破之。

translations.literal: Shen as Qi minister of state struck Chen Xi’s general Zhang Chun and destroyed him.
translations.idiomatic: As Qi’s minister of state Cao Shen defeated Chen Xi’s general Zhang Chun.

sentences.zh: 黥布反，參從悼惠王將車騎十二萬，與高祖會擊黥布軍，大破之。

translations.literal: When Qing Bu rebelled, Shen followed Prince Dao Hui of Qi in commanding a hundred twenty thousand chariots and horse, joining Gaozu in striking Qing Bu’s army and smashing it greatly.
translations.idiomatic: When Qing Bu rose, Cao Shen led a hundred twenty thousand chariots and horse for Prince Liu Fei (Daohui), joined Gaozu, and shattered Qing Bu’s army.

sentences.zh: 南至蘄，還定竹邑、相、蕭、留。

translations.literal: South he reached Qi, returning to pacify Zhuyi, Xiang, Xiao, and Liu.
translations.idiomatic: He drove south to the Chu county of Qi, then on the withdrawal secured Zhuyi, Xiang, Xiao, and Liu.

sentences.zh: 參功：凡下二國，縣百二十二；

translations.literal: Shen’s achievements: in all he reduced two states, a hundred twenty-two counties;
translations.idiomatic: The tally of Cao Shen’s campaigns: he overthrew two kingdoms and reduced 122 counties;

sentences.zh: 得王二人，相三人，將軍六人，大莫囂、郡守、司馬、候、御史各一人。

translations.literal: He captured two kings, three chancellors, six generals, and one each of grand Mo’ao, commandery governor, major, marquis (hou), and imperial clerk.
translations.idiomatic: he took two kings, three chancellors, six generals, and one each of the Chu grand office of Mo’ao, commandery governor, commandant, marquis-rank officer, and censor.

sentences.zh: 始參微時，與蕭何善，及為宰相，有隙。

translations.literal: At first when Shen was lowly he was on good terms with Xiao He; when they came to be chancellors there was a rift.
translations.idiomatic: In obscurity Cao Shen had been close to Xiao He; once both held the chancellorship they fell out.

sentences.zh: 至何且死，所推賢唯參。

translations.literal: When He was about to die, the worthy he recommended was only Shen.
translations.idiomatic: Yet on his deathbed Xiao He’s sole recommendation was Cao Shen.

sentences.zh: 參代何為相國，舉事無所變更，壹遵何之約束。

translations.literal: Shen replaced He as minister of state; in initiating affairs he made no changes, uniformly following He’s regulations.
translations.idiomatic: Cao Shen succeeded Xiao He and altered nothing—he enforced Xiao He’s rules to the letter.

sentences.zh: 擇郡國吏長大，訥於文辭，謹厚長者，即召除為丞相史。

translations.literal: He chose among commandery and kingdom clerks those who were tall and grown, slow of speech and lacking in rhetorical flourish, careful, thick, and elder-like, and immediately summoned and appointed them as secretary clerks of the chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: He filled the chancellery with stolid local officers—men of few words, plain style, and steady habit.

sentences.zh: 吏言文刻深，欲務聲名，輒斥去之。

translations.literal: Those clerks whose speech was refined, cutting, and deep, who desired to pursue reputation, he immediately drove away.
translations.idiomatic: Slick rhetoricians angling for fame he dismissed at once.

sentences.zh: 日夜飲酒。

translations.literal: Day and night he drank ale.
translations.idiomatic: He passed his days and nights in his cups.

sentences.zh: 卿大夫以下吏及賓客見參不事事，來者皆欲有言。

translations.literal: From grand ministers down, clerks and guests who saw Shen not doing affairs—those who came all wished to speak.
translations.idiomatic: From senior ministers down, staff and visitors saw the chief minister idle and came hoping to remonstrate.

sentences.zh: 至者，參輒飲以醇酒，度之欲有言，復飲酒，醉而後去，終莫得開說，以為常。

translations.literal: Those who arrived, Shen would give strong ale to drink; guessing they wished to speak, again gave ale to drink, and only when drunk let them leave, so in the end none could get to open persuasion—this became the norm.
translations.idiomatic: He plied each with strong wine; if they tried to speak he poured again until they reeled home—so no one ever got a word in, and that became routine.

sentences.zh: 相舍後園近吏舍，吏舍日飲歌呼。

translations.literal: The chancellor’s residence back garden was near the clerks’ quarters; the clerks’ quarters daily drank and sang and shouted.
translations.idiomatic: The chancellery garden backed onto the clerks’ dormitory, where they drank and whooped every night.

sentences.zh: 從吏患之，無如何，乃請參遊後園。

translations.literal: The attending clerks worried about it, could do nothing about it, therefore requested Shen to stroll in the back garden.
translations.idiomatic: His aides fretted but could not silence them, so they asked Cao Shen to tour the garden.

sentences.zh: 聞吏醉歌呼，從吏幸相國召按之。

translations.literal: Hearing the clerks drunkenly singing and shouting, the attending clerks hoped the minister of state would summon and investigate them.
translations.idiomatic: They hoped he would hear the noise and order a crackdown.

sentences.zh: 乃反取酒張坐飲，大歌呼與相和。

translations.literal: Then instead he fetched ale, spread mats and sat drinking, greatly singing and shouting in harmony with them.
translations.idiomatic: Instead he brought out wine, sat among them, and joined the roar.

sentences.zh: 參見人之有細過，掩匿覆蓋之，府中無事。

translations.literal: When Shen saw men have petty faults, he covered and concealed them; in the headquarters there were no troubles.
translations.idiomatic: He smoothed over small mistakes so that the ministry stayed free of scandal.

sentences.zh: 參子窋為中大夫。

translations.literal: Shen’s son Kui was a palace counselor.
translations.idiomatic: His son Cao Kui held the post of palace counselor.

sentences.zh: 惠帝怪相國不治事，以為「豈少朕與？」

translations.literal: Emperor Hui wondered at the minister of state not governing affairs, thinking “Does he belittle Us?”
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Hui wondered whether his chief minister’s idleness meant he scorned a young ruler.

sentences.zh: 乃謂窋曰：「女歸，試私從容問乃父曰：『高帝新棄群臣，帝富於春秋，君為相國，日飲，無所請事，何以憂天下？』

translations.literal: He therefore said to Kui, “You return; try privately and at ease to ask your father, saying, ‘The High Emperor has newly left the ministers; the Emperor is rich in spring and autumn; you as minister of state daily drink, request nothing in the way of affairs—how do you worry for the empire?’
translations.idiomatic: He told Cao Kui, “At home, casually ask your father: ‘The Founding Emperor is gone, the Son of Heaven is still young; you do nothing but drink—how does that show care for the realm?’

sentences.zh: 然無言吾告女也。」

translations.literal: But do not say that I told you.”
translations.idiomatic: Say nothing of my instructions.”

sentences.zh: 窋既洗沐歸，時間，自從其所諫參。

translations.literal: Kui having bathed and returned, at the appropriate time, from his own standpoint remonstrated with Shen.
translations.idiomatic: After his ritual bath Cao Kui waited for a private moment and spoke as the emperor had coached him.

sentences.zh: 參怒而笞之二百，曰：「趣入侍，天下事非乃所當言也。」

translations.literal: Shen in anger flogged him two hundred strokes, saying, “Hasten to enter and serve; affairs of the empire are not what you ought to speak of.”
translations.idiomatic: Cao Shen thrashed him two hundred strokes with the rod and barked, “Back to your post—state business is not yours to discuss.”

sentences.zh: 至朝時，帝讓參曰：「與窋胡治乎？

translations.literal: When court arrived, Emperor Hui reproached Shen, saying, “What dealing did you have with Kui?
translations.idiomatic: At the next audience Emperor Hui reproached him: “Why did you beat Cao Kui?

sentences.zh: 乃者我使諫君也。」

translations.literal: Recently I had him remonstrate with you.”
translations.idiomatic: I sent him to reason with you.”

sentences.zh: 參免冠謝曰：「陛下自察聖武孰與高皇帝？」

translations.literal: Shen removed his cap and apologized, saying, “May Your Majesty examine yourself: in sagely martial prowess, how do you compare to the High Emperor?”
translations.idiomatic: Cao Shen doffed his cap and asked, “Set your sage strength beside the Late Emperor’s—which is greater?”

sentences.zh: 上曰：「朕乃安敢望先帝！」

translations.literal: The Sovereign said, “How would We dare look up to the Late Emperor!”
translations.idiomatic: “I dare not compare myself to him,” said the emperor.

sentences.zh: 參曰：「陛下觀參孰與蕭何賢？」

translations.literal: Shen said, “Your Majesty observe Shen: how does he compare to Xiao He in worth?”
translations.idiomatic: “And how do I measure against Xiao He?” asked Cao Shen.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「君似不及也。」

translations.literal: The Sovereign said, “You seem not to reach him.”
translations.idiomatic: “You fall short,” the emperor admitted.

sentences.zh: 參曰：「陛下言之是也。

translations.literal: Shen said, “What Your Majesty says is correct.
translations.idiomatic: “Then we agree,” said Cao Shen.

sentences.zh: 且高皇帝與蕭何定天下，法令既明具，陛下垂拱，參等守職，遵而勿失，不亦可乎？」

translations.literal: Moreover the High Emperor with Xiao He fixed the empire; laws and orders are already clear and complete; Your Majesty hangs down the sash; we such as Shen guard office, follow and do not lose—is this not also acceptable?”
translations.idiomatic: The Late Emperor and Xiao He set the laws in clear order. Your Majesty has only to fold your hands on the throne; we ministers need only hold the line without blunder—is that not enough?”

sentences.zh: 惠帝曰：「善。

translations.literal: Emperor Hui said, “Good.
translations.idiomatic: “Well said,” said Emperor Hui.

sentences.zh: 君休矣！」

translations.literal: Sir, rest yourself!”
translations.idiomatic: “Let the matter drop.”

sentences.zh: 窋嗣侯，高后時至御史大夫。

translations.literal: Kui succeeded to the marquisate; in Empress Gao’s time he reached imperial censor in chief.
translations.idiomatic: Cao Kui inherited the title and under Empress Lü rose to imperial censor in chief.

sentences.zh: 傳國至曾孫襄，武帝時為將軍，擊匈奴，薨。

translations.literal: The state passed to great-grandson Xiang, who under Emperor Wu was a general, struck the Xiongnu, and died.
translations.idiomatic: The line passed to great-grandson Xiang, who under Emperor Wu campaigned against the Xiongnu as a general and died.

sentences.zh: 子宗嗣，有罪，完為城旦。

translations.literal: His son Zong succeeded; for a crime he was sentenced to wan-status dawn wall-builder labor.
translations.idiomatic: His son Zong inherited but was convicted and sentenced to dawn corvée as a wall-builder under the wan punishment that spared mutilation.

sentences.zh: 至哀帝時，乃封參玄孫之孫本始為平陽侯，二千戶，王莽時薨。

translations.literal: Under Emperor Ai, then his collateral great-great-grandson’s grandson Benshi was enfeoffed as Marquis of Pingyang, two thousand households; he died in Wang Mang’s time.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Ai the court enfeoffed Benshi—descended from a collateral branch—with two thousand households at Pingyang; he died in Wang Mang’s day.

sentences.zh: 子宏嗣，建武中先降河北，封平陽侯。

translations.literal: His son Hong succeeded; in the Jianwu period he first surrendered north of the He and was enfeoffed Marquis of Pingyang.
translations.idiomatic: His son Hong submitted early to the Hebei armies in the Jianwu era and was again named Marquis of Pingyang.

sentences.zh: 至今八侯。

translations.literal: Down to the present eight marquises.
translations.idiomatic: By the compilers’ time the house had produced eight marquises.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：蕭何、曹參皆起秦刀筆吏，當時錄錄未有奇節。

translations.literal: The encomium says: Xiao He and Cao Shen both rose from Qin brush-and-knife clerks; at that time they were undistinguished and had no extraordinary integrity.
translations.idiomatic: The summation runs: Xiao He and Cao Shen began as humble Qin clerks—ordinary men without romantic legend.

sentences.zh: 漢興，依日月之末光，何以信謹守管籥，參與韓信俱征伐。

translations.literal: When Han rose, relying on the last rays of sun and moon, He thereby faithfully and carefully guarded the keys; Shen joined Han Xin together in campaigns of conquest.
translations.idiomatic: When the Han rose they caught the founder’s reflected glory: Xiao He faithfully held the bolts of state while Cao Shen marched with Han Xin.

sentences.zh: 天下既定，因民之疾秦法，順流與之更始，二人同心，遂安海內。

translations.literal: When the empire was already fixed, because the people loathed Qin law, they went with the current and with them made a new beginning; the two men were of one heart, thereupon the seas within were pacified.
translations.idiomatic: After victory they rode popular hatred of Qin’s laws to begin anew; of one mind they brought peace within the seas.

sentences.zh: 淮陰、黥布等已滅，唯何、參擅功名，位冠群臣，聲施後世，為一代之宗臣，慶流曲裔，盛矣哉！

translations.literal: When Huaiyin, Qing Bu, and the rest were already extinguished, only He and Shen monopolized achievement and fame, their position topping the host of ministers, their fame extending to later ages, becoming pillar ministers of a generation, blessing flowing to bending descendants—how splendid!
translations.idiomatic: With the Marquis of Huaiyin, Qing Bu, and the like gone, only Xiao He and Cao Shen towered above the ministers, their names lasting as the dynasty’s defining pillars, their fortune reaching even distant twigs of the clan—magnificent indeed!
